en.title: TypeScript Literal Types
en.description: Literal Types in TypeScript restrict variables to specific values, ensuring more precise and safer code.

content.text: Literal Types in TypeScript allow a variable to hold only specific values, such as a particular string or number. They are often used with `string`, `number`, or `boolean` values.

Literal Types are defined using the `:` symbol. For example, `let status: "active" | "inactive";` restricts the variable to only `"active"` or `"inactive"`. Using an invalid value triggers a TypeScript warning. See code examples below.

items.description: Variables are restricted to specific values.
Literal types can be combined with `|` and other types.
Parameters or return values can be restricted with literal types.
Object properties can use literal types.

content.text:
- **Dangers**: Too many literal types can complicate code. Restricting to incorrect values may cause errors.
- **Recommendations**: Use simple and meaningful literal types, combine correctly with union types, and avoid `any`.
